content:
First, observe that if \(n\) has \(k\) digits then the maximum of the left‐hand side, 
\[
P(n)\;=\;\prod\text{(digits of }n),
\]
is \(9^k\).  Meanwhile for large \(n\), 
\[
n^2-15n-27\sim n^2,
\]
which eventually far exceeds \(9^k\).  In fact one checks:

1.  One‐digit \(n\le9\) gives  
   \(P(n)=n\) and the equation 
   \[
     n = n^2-15n-27
     \;\Longrightarrow\;
     n^2-16n-27=0
   \]
   has no integer root in \(\{1,\dots,9\}\).

2.  Two‐digit \(n=10a+b\) has \(P(n)=ab\).  A few inequalities show that if
   \[
     n^2-15n-27 \;>\; 9\cdot9=81
   \]
   then no solution is possible, and
   \[
     n^2-15n-27\ge0
     \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
     n\ge17.
   \]
   Hence one need only check \(n=17,18,19,20\):
   
   \(n=17:\quad P(17)=1\cdot7=7,\)
   
   \(\quad17^2-15\cdot17-27=289-255-27=7,\)  ✓

   \(n=18:\;P(18)=8,\quad18^2-15\cdot18-27=324-270-27=27,\)  no  
   
   \(n=19:\;P(19)=9,\quad19^2-15\cdot19-27=361-285-27=49,\)  no  
   
   \(n=20:\;P(20)=0,\quad20^2-15\cdot20-27=400-300-27=73,\)  no.

All larger \(n\) fail because \(n^2-15n-27\) grows past the maximum digit‐product.  Hence the only solution is \(n=17\), and the sum of all solutions is

\[
\boxed{17}.
\]
